narrative:
Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated a 63.5% chance that you would not pass. This prediction stems from a careful analysis of key elements in your profile, like your undergraduate GPA of 3.00, LSAT score of 36, Year 1 and Year 3 grade deciles, family income in the middle bracket, and your plan to attend full-time. The model flagged your overall academic standing as a concern, particularly since students who passed the bar typically had stronger Year 1 deciles around the 6th level and Year 3 deciles near the 6th as well, while yours sat at 2nd and 1st respectively—putting you below those benchmarks.

The most influential factor pushing the prediction toward bar failure was your Year 3 grade decile, as it stood out as notably lower than what the model associates with success, signaling potential challenges in sustaining performance over time. Next in importance was your LSAT score, which actually worked in your favor by pulling the prediction somewhat toward passing, though not enough to offset other concerns since it was a bit below the average of 37.27 for those who succeeded. Your Year 1 grade decile also contributed toward the failure prediction, reinforcing the sense of early academic hurdles compared to passers' averages.

While your middle family income aligns with over a third of successful students and your full-time status matches nearly all of them, these didn't carry enough weight to change the outcome. In contrast to typical accepted applicants—who often show higher deciles, LSATs above 37, and GPAs around 3.24—your profile clustered more closely with patterns the model links to first-try bar struggles. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background as a Native American woman pursuing law full-time; it's purely the model's data-driven read on bar passage odds.

To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your recent grades through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that highlight your resilience and growth. Many students in similar positions improve these metrics and succeed on reapplication—reach out to admissions advisors for targeted guidance on building a stronger case next cycle. With focused effort, you can shift your profile toward the successful patterns the model recognizes.
